key_insight: Adding site-projected orbital density of states (pDOS) fingerprints to graph neural network node features significantly improves materials property prediction by injecting local electronic-structure information beyond static elemental descriptors.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical way to boost accuracy for crystal-property prediction in computational materials design, especially when first-principles electronic information is available and high-quality labels are limited.
method: Encode each atomic site with pDOS fingerprints from DFT plus Pettifor elemental embeddings.
Fuse these node features before message passing in a graph neural network.
Use spectral attention gating to identify which orbitals and energy windows matter most for each target property.
evidence_signal: Compared with an elemental-descriptor baseline, pDOS augmentation reduced prediction error by 22.9% for superconducting critical temperature and 27.9% for optical dielectric constant, with gains comparable to doubling the training set.
watch_item: The benefit depends on dataset size and may shrink for smaller datasets when the pDOS feature length is not well supported by the available training data.
paper_title: Machine Learning Materials Properties by Encoding Orbital-Projected Density of States
